Tafseer:
Expression of the importance of the Surah:
This Surah, as we have pointed out, holds the status of a completion and complement of Surah Al-Mu'minoon. For this reason, without any specific introduction, it begins with a mere warning. The omission of the subject (mubtada), as we have explained in its place, is a clue that the entire attention of the addressee is intended to be concentrated on the predicate (khabar), from which the importance and majesty of this Surah is shown. That is: this is a majestic Surah which We have sent down. "We have sent down"—meaning, do not consider it a light thing and an empty word, but this is Our sent-down speech and Our revealed decree. For this reason, compliance with its every word must be done as Our obligatory decree. "Wa faradnaha" (and We have made it obligatory)—meaning, the commands and directions in it hold the status of obligations; let everyone obey them without hesitation, and let not carelessness and indifference find any way in any matter.

Pointer to the warnings of the Surah:
"Wa anzalna feeha ayatin bayyinatin la'allakum tadhakkaroon" (and We have sent down in it clear verses that perhaps you will remember)—this is a pointer to those warnings which, along with the description of commands and directions, have entered repeatedly like a refrain in this Surah. For example, see verses 10, 14, 17, 20, 34, 46, and 58. The common purpose of these warnings is: adhere to these commands being given with complete attention, otherwise remember that God's grasp is highly severe. Within this Surah, the need for these warnings specifically was because the commands given in it regarding family, society, punishments, and limits became a place of slipping for the past nations, because of which those nations fell under God's wrath. Upon this Ummah is the great favor and mercy of Allah that along with the commands, He has informed them of the consequences of their violation repeatedly, so that people may remain fully awake and alert. To this, "la'allakum tadhakkaroon" (that perhaps you will remember) points.

To the disorder and corruption of society, the greatest part is held by fornication/adultery (zina), because the stability of society depends on the purity of the womb relation and its being safe from every kind of disruption and corruption, and fornication destroys the purity of this relation to put society on the path of animalistic disorder, whose result ultimately is: the entire society, instead of being a pure society, becomes a herd of beasts. Since this disruption and disorder uproots the foundation of civilized life, in all heavenly religions, fornication has been established as a crime worthy of punishment. Islam also gave commands from the very first stage to stop this disorder. Consequently, in verses 15-16 of Surah An-Nisa, the initial commands of this chain were given when situations were not yet helpful for the execution of limits and punishments, and along with it, pointer was made that the decisive and final commands would be sent down later. Consequently, by this verse, that promise was fulfilled. For both the female fornicator and the male fornicator, the command was given that each of them be whipped a hundred lashes.

Indifference in the execution of limits is contrary to faith:
"Wa la ta'khudhkum bihima ra'fatun fee deeni Allahi in kuntum tu'minoona billahi wal-yawmi al-akhiri" (And let not pity for them take you in the religion of Allah, if you should believe in Allah and the Last Day)—by "deen" (religion) here is meant this very limit described for fornication. That is: in the execution of it, let no softness, indifference, or turning away find any path. Neither should any softness be shown to the woman, nor to the man, nor to the rich, nor to the poor; the unbiased execution of God's prescribed limits without any favor is the inevitable requirement of faith in Allah and faith in the Hereafter. Those who show indifference and softness in this matter—their faith in Allah and the Hereafter is not credible. Here, this thing too is worthy of attention: in the description of the punishment, the mention of the woman is advanced over the mention of the man. The reason for this, while being that fornication cannot occur without the woman's consent, is also that because of her being the weaker sex, the possibility of the feeling of sympathy arising is greater. For this reason, the Quran advanced her mention here so that through the style of expression itself, this point may be clarified: in this matter, there is no favor for anyone in the presence of Allah, whether woman or man.

The indifference of the Jews in the matter of limits:
In the matter of limits, the need for this emphasis and warning was because the Jews had practically dropped most limits. The punishment of fornication in their Shariah was stoning (rajm), but practically the state was: if some poor person was caught in this crime, this punishment was executed on him; but if some rich person committed it, they did not address him. And now in the current times, it has become an independent philosophy: those who commit crimes do so because of some mental sickness; for this reason, they are worthy of training, reform, and sympathy, not of some severe punishment. The blessing of this philosophy is: the earth of God has become filled with ruffians and scoundrels, and the life and honor of no decent person has remained safe from their hands. Take your own country. If you mention this punishment of fornication mentioned above before the directors of your law, despite their tall claims of faith, they will call it barbaric; and if because of interest they do not call it barbaric, they anyway consider it barbaric. But the state of the country is: without exaggeration, thousands of lives and honors are destroyed every year at the hands of scoundrels in such a cruel manner that reading their news every morning in the newspapers makes the heart break.

For thieves and fornicators, the state of these gentlemen's feeling of pity and sympathy is: they have become more kind to them, as it were, than the Merciful God Himself—that their hearts tremble at the thought of cutting their hands and whipping them—but the families who are falling victim to murder, looting, and disgrace at the hands of these thieves and scoundrels every day—their hearts do not melt at their oppression in the least!

The blessing of Islamic limits and punishments:
If these gentlemen do not feel any need to maintain the honor of their claim of faith, let them obtain some lesson at least from the experiences of the world. On this very earth, the governments of Najd, Hijaz, and Yemen are also present. In them, Islamic limits and punishments are executed. In this era of statistics, it can easily be known: how many events of theft, robbery, dacoity, and fornication occurred in their countries, and how many thieves' hands were cut, and how many fornicators were whipped. I do not possess the statistics, but the statements of eyewitnesses are in my knowledge, and my personal observation too: neither is there the existence of crimes there, nor of criminals. This is merely the awe of Islamic limits and punishments: neither do events of theft and fornication occur there, nor does the turn to cut thieves' hands and whip fornicators arrive. If some rare event does occur occasionally, the punishment the criminal receives is sufficient for the lesson-learning of the public. In contrast to it, when we cast a look at the state of our country, we have to say in the words of our master Jesus: "You have made my Father's house a den of thieves and robbers."

An important purpose of Islamic limits and punishments:
"Wal-yashhad 'adhabahuma ta'ifatun mina al-mu'mineen" (And let a group of the believers witness their punishment)—an important purpose of Islamic limits and punishments is: others should obtain lesson and advice from them. Consequently, in the Quran, they are expressed as "nakal" (exemplary punishment), which means a lesson-giving punishment. The demand of this interest was: these punishments be given in the presence of a group of Muslims. If these punishments are given silently in the cells of jails, this interest of theirs is lost. "Ta'ifah" means group and assembly; according to some commentators, this word applies also to a single person. It is not known what the source of this rare research of these gentlemen is!

The predicate in the meaning of prohibition:
"La yankihu" is used here not in the meaning of predicate, but in the meaning of prohibition. When inside the prohibition, along with the prohibition, the meaning of direction and advice is also present, it enters in the form of a predicate. The explanation of this style has passed under Al-Baqarah 272. Here, the purpose is to awaken the sense of faith of the Muslim society: "Inside you, there must be such hatred and aversion to fornication that if any fornicator wants to marry among you, no believer should be ready to give his daughter to him. If he is to find anyone for marriage, let him find only a female fornicator or a polytheist; no believing woman should be pleased to give herself into his marriage-contract. Similarly, if there is a female fornicator, no believer should marry her. If she is to find anyone for marriage, let her find only a male fornicator or a polytheist; no Muslim should be pleased to bring this impurity into his house." It is said: "Wa hurrima dhalika 'ala al-mu'mineen" (and that has been made unlawful for the believers)—marriage-relation with male and female fornicators is unlawful for the believers.

The mutual similarity between fornication and Shirk:
In the verse, the simultaneous mention of male fornicator and polytheist, and female fornicator and polytheist, is highly meaningful. On the mutual similarity of fornication and Shirk, we have written in places in this book. In ancient heavenly scrolls, the polytheist is compared to a whoring woman. We have explained the eloquence of this comparison in its place. Fornication is the same kind of moral impurity as Shirk is the impurity of beliefs. In the Quran, because of this characteristic sharing, Shirk and polytheists are called impure (najis) too.

The need to awaken the sense of faith of the Muslim society:
The need to awaken the sense of faith of the society was because mere law, no matter how unbiased and wise it may be, cannot protect society until hatred for evil is present inside society itself. For the execution of every law, particularly when it is a strict law, there are inevitably such conditions that many criminals succeed in saving themselves from its grasp. Dealing with such criminals is the responsibility of society itself: that it should not give place to such people in its eyes; rather, wherever they stay, they are forced to live as outcasts. If such people are desirous of a relationship with some decent Muslim, he should reject their request, to the extent that such people cannot even dare to seek relationship in some decent family. This alertness is necessary for stopping crimes. Among the calamities of this era, not only this is a calamity that limits and punishments are not Islamic, but a greater calamity than this is that people do not possess the Islamic sense. In this era, if anyone searches for his daughter, he searches for all those attributes in him which are considered necessary from the aspect of worldly prestige, but very few people have remained now who keep in view that aspect of the matter to which direction has been given in the verse.

Verses 4-5:
The law of testimony and punishment in the case of Qadhf (false accusation):
Now, the law of testimony and punishment in the case of Qadhf is being described: those who accuse chaste and free believing women of fornication—it is necessary for them to present four eyewitnesses of the crime. If they cannot present four credible witnesses, then the command is: whip them eighty lashes, and declare them disqualified from giving testimony forever. It should be kept in mind: in Muslim society, someone being declared disqualified from giving testimony forever is no small thing; rather, inside society, this is equivalent to the complete end of his social standing. The primary status of this Ummah is: (that you may be witnesses over mankind). If some person is declared disqualified from giving testimony, it means: he has lost that primary honor which was granted to him as an individual of this Ummah.

"Alladhina", although comes for masculine, if any woman commits this crime, there is no reason why she should be exempt from this command; for this reason, this word will be general for both woman and man by way of dominance.

The word "muhsanat", in such a context, comes for free and decent women in contrast to slave-girls; for this reason, those who are in favor of half the punishment of this for slave-girls and slaves have a statement based on argument.

After repentance and reform, in the sight of Allah, the sins of such people will indeed be forgiven, but as far as being disqualified from giving testimony is concerned, this thing will remain. This is because repentance relates to the heart of man, and the state of the heart is known to Allah alone. From the words of the verse too, this point emerges: forgiveness relates only to repentance. Testimony has no connection with it; they will remain disqualified from giving testimony forever (abada).

Verses 6-9:
"Walladhina yarmoona azwajahum wa lam yakun lahum shuhada'u illa anfusuhum fashahadatu ahadihim arba'u shahadatin billahi innahu lamina as-sadiqeen. Wal-khamisatu anna la'nata Allahi 'alayhi in kana mina al-kadhibeen. Wa yadra'u 'anha al-'adhaba an tashhada arba'a shahadatin billahi innahu lamina al-kadhibeen. Wal-khamisatu anna ghadaba Allahi 'alayha in kana mina as-sadiqeen."

The regulation of Li'an (mutual oath of condemnation):
If a person accuses his wife of fornication, and he has no witnesses except his own self, then in this case, the decision of the matter will be made by oath. This is called Li'an in the terminology of the Shariah. Its form will be: the man will swear four times by Allah that he is truthful in the accusation he is making. The fifth time, he will say: if he is making a false accusation, let the curse of Allah be upon him. If the woman does not make any defense against this oath of the man, the same limit of fornication will be executed on her which was mentioned above; and if she does not accept this accusation, her release from the punishment will be when she swears four times by Allah that this man is a liar, and the fifth time, says: let the wrath of Allah be upon her if this man is telling the truth.

A warning:
This verse, as pointed out in the first verse, serves as a warning and reminder, and in it, the reply to the condition (jawab ash-shart) is assumed as omitted, in accordance with the well-known rule of Arabic. Forward in verse 14, this omission has also been opened. The meaning is: this is the blessing of Allah's favor, His mercy, and His being Accepting of Repentance and Wise—that He is giving you these clear directions and explicit, wise commands to open the path of repentance and reform for you, otherwise, the path you had set out on was inviting the wrath of God.

It should be kept in mind here: these commands were sent down in that era when among the Muslims, through the conspiracies of the hypocrites, some highly severe, as detailed forward, weaknesses had manifested. But Allah, instead of giving punishment on these weaknesses, by His favor and mercy, made them the cause of the sending down of these commands and directions which were necessary for protecting the Muslim society from the conspiracies and mischief-makings of the devils and hypocrites.

The upcoming theme... Verses 11-26:
Forward, pointing to the trial of Ifk (the slander), attention is directed toward those weaknesses which manifested from a group of Muslims on this occasion—meaning, they too joined in the trial raised by the hypocrites without realizing it, and began to utter the same kind of words which the hypocrites wanted to spread. Whereas, the basis of the mutual relationship of Muslims must be on trust and perfect good-thinking. If any Muslim hears any bad thing regarding his other brother or sister, he must not accept it and bring it on his tongue until its complete investigation is done. Within this connection, the desire and effort of the hypocrites and their patrons to spread various rumors to uproot the moral reputation of Muslims has been exposed; for this reason, they must be fully alert to them, and must not themselves provide the means for their objective. For this purpose, the necessary directions are also given in this connection, and direction is also given to pardon those who fell into this trial without realizing it... In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

The trial of Ifk (the slander):
"Ifk" refers to a false, fabricated, self-carved, and contrary-to-reality statement. Here, by it is pointed to that trial which the hypocrites raised on the occasion of the expedition of Bani Mustaliq to accuse the Mother of the Believers, Hazrat Aisha as-Siddiqah (may Allah be pleased with her). Some lies are such that even bringing their mention on the tongue is not tolerated, and this lie was fabricated regarding the mother of all Muslims; for this reason, the Quran pointed to it by the word "Ifk" and ignored it. It only gave the information: who the inventors and spreaders of this lie are, so that it may be estimated: the statement spread from this corrupt source is neither worthy of mention in itself, nor worthy of consideration and investigation. Consequently, the Quran did not point to the nature of the event itself. From this, it appears: among the wise Muslims, no hesitation was found regarding this trial of the hypocrites being a fabrication and slander. They knew that this was a trial raised by the hypocrites, but when some sensation-creating statement is spread among the common folk, those who are not very mature in understanding begin to relate and transmit it without understanding. [1]

Pointer to the hypocrites:
"'Usbatun minkum" (a group from among you) points to that group of the hypocrites which was within the Muslims. Their leader was Abdullah bin Ubayy; because in their apparent conduct, these people were not separate from the Muslims, they obtained the opportunity to enter among the Muslims to spread their poison, and many simple-minded Muslims fell for their deceptions. Here, the Quran has raised the finger toward them by saying "'usbatun minkum", and the purpose of this pointer is to alert the Muslims: "This is a trial raised by a group within yourselves; be alert to them."

Aspects of good inside this trial:
"La tahsaboohu sharran lakum bal huwa khayrun lakum"—meaning, these wicked ones raising this trial has not been bad for you; rather, if you benefit from it correctly, there are many aspects of good in it for you.

The prominent aspect of good inside it was: through it, the Muslims obtained the opportunity to recognize that group of hypocrites inside themselves well. If this opportunity had not been created, who knows what shape this cancer inside would have taken, and what corruptions would have manifested from it!

The second aspect of good in it was: the weaknesses which were hidden inside the Muslims arose through this trial, and their reform and treatment were done. From the perspective of the purification of society, its importance is completely clear.

The third aspect of good in it is: this event created a highly helpful atmosphere for sending down many commands and directions regarding the reform and organization of society. If these commands had been sent down without this atmosphere, their true value would not have been clear to many.

"Likulli imri'in minhum ma iktasaba mina al-ithmi walladhee tawalla kibrahu minhum lahu 'adhabun 'azeem"—"kibr" is used for the primary and major part of some crime or sin. "Alladhee tawalla kibrahu"—meaning, he who became the primary برپا-maker of this trial; this is a pointer to Abdullah bin Ubayy. This was an extremely wicked hypocrite. Before this too, he had raised multiple trials, and the entire responsibility for this trial was principally upon him. In the above segment, there was comfort for the believers: for you, this trial has been a cause of good in every aspect; however, for these wicked ones, it was a cause of destruction. Whomever of them took part in this trial according to his degree will suffer its punishment according to his degree, and for him who is the actual water of this entire storm, there is a great punishment in the Hereafter.

[1] From the books of history and biography, the nature of the event is known only as: the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was returning to Madinah from the expedition of Bani Mustaliq (occurred in 6 AH). Hazrat Aisha as-Siddiqah was traveling on a separate camel. During the night, the army camped. Before the army departed, the Mother of the Believers went out for necessity. Meanwhile, the command to depart was given. The caravan departed, and the driver of the Mother of the Believers also departed, assuming she was inside her litter. When the Mother of the Believers returned to the camp and saw that the caravan had departed, she saw no plan in the night except to stay there, so that Allah might open some way. Hazrat Safwan, a companion, was appointed to follow the caravan to look for any forgotten things. When he arrived at the camp in the morning and saw that the Mother of the Believers was left behind, he recited "Inna lillahi...". Ultimately, he made his camel sit. The Mother of the Believers rode on it, and he held the rein to catch up with the caravan. During the departure and stay of an army, such an occurrence is no extraordinary thing. But the hypocrites turned this minor thing into a tale.

The moral principle of Islamic society:
In Muslim society, every Muslim man and woman has the right that other members of society keep good-thinking (husn-dan) regarding them, and respect this right of theirs until it is proved by argument that they are no longer worthy of this good-thinking. The inevitable requirement of this right is: if any Muslim hears any such thing regarding his other Muslim brother or sister which deprives them of this good-thinking, he should not accept it as a gift and begin to relate and transmit it; rather, he should reject it, and not believe it until some reasonable proof of it comes before him. In such matters, the attitude of neutrality is also not permissible—meaning, that a person considers it an external dispute, ignoring it, letting the other be defamed, thinking: what do I have to do with this dispute! Rather, as far as possible, he must defend his brother, because protecting the honor and respect of his other Muslim brother is an obligation upon every Muslim. On this moral principle of Islamic society, He has warned the Muslims here: when this word fell into your ears, why did you not keep good-thinking regarding one another, and clearly announce: "This is a clear slander and an explicit accusation."

The corruption of our society's temperament:
On one hand, keep this moral principle of Islamic society in view, and on the other hand, review your own society; you will realize: today, completely the opposite principle is active in it. Today, the theory is: every person is worthy of bad-thinking (soo'-dan), unless some personal or group interest of one's own is associated with him. And spreading sensation-creating rumors regarding others has become an independent art and a highly successful profession in this era. How many writers are there in our nation whose profession is only this: they search for such rumors every morning to write them down! These rumors are published under highly prominent headlines in newspapers and magazines, and the most successful newspapers and magazines are those which are the most clever in inventing such rumors. The state of the society's corruption of temperament is: people read such things, and let alone saying "Hadha ifkun mubeen" (This is a clear slander), they say "We believe and testify" to their every word, and accept them as revelation, relating and transmitting them.

Demand of four witnesses for the proof of accusation:
That is, the words of these fabricators could be worthy of consideration only if, in accordance with the law prescribed by the Shariah, they had presented four witnesses in proof of their accusation. It should be clear that the law of four witnesses for the proof of such an accusation had been sent down long before this in verse 15 of Surah An-Nisa. The meaning is: when these corrupt ones made this accusation, their legal and religious responsibility was to present four witnesses, but when they could not present four witnesses, by the law of God, they are the liars. Then, those who believed their word without proof being provided from their side—on what authority did they believe it!

Warning to the Muslims again:
"Afada fee al-hadithi" means: making one word from another, a person goes from here to there and makes a mountain out of a molehill. After verse 10, this is another warning to the Muslims: "In this matter, the path you had set out on demanded that a great punishment should grasp you, but upon you is Allah's favor and His mercy in this world and the Hereafter, because of which He pardoned." The meaning is: appreciate this favor and mercy of Allah, lest you fall into some similar trial again to make yourselves worthy of punishment.

This address is to the entire society, although those involved in this matter or those who let carelessness and indifference find way were not many. The reason for this general address is: the nature of such corruptions is like epidemic diseases. If its manifestation occurs even in some individuals, every person must be warned of the danger, lest this epidemic become common and then, in its consequence, the entire society come under God's grasp.

Direction for caution in the matter of transmission and relation:
This is the description of the severity of that matter which some people, considering it a light thing, began to relate and transmit without hesitation, and they did not think: what they are considering a light thing is a highly severe matter in the presence of Allah. The actual path to accept any statement is the intellect, not the tongue; and worthy of transmission and relation is that statement whose basis is on credible knowledge, not on rumor. If a person suspends his intellect, and has only a yard-long tongue, he will begin to relate every word falling into his ears without criticism, and in this manner, he himself will fall into the trial spread by Satan, and will put others into it too. In this verse, the Muslims are warned of this danger: in this matter, some people made only the tongue the medium of receiving the relation instead of the intellect, and began to relate a mere rumor instead of knowledge. They considered this matter a light thing, whereas in terms of its results and consequences, this thing is highly dangerous and a great crime in the presence of Allah.

"Subhanaka" (Holy are You) here is used in the exact same meaning as we say "Ma'adh Allah" (God forbid). The meaning is: when such a statement came before you which was completely devoid of proof and testimony, and whose blow fell not only on a believing woman's honor but on the Mother of the Believers' honor, why did you not say on hearing it: "We have no right to speak such a word regarding any believing woman without proof and testimony. God forbid! This is a clear slander!"

From these verses, those principles which emerge regarding transmission, critical analysis, and the acceptance of relation are highly important. If there were no fear of the discussion exceeding its limits, we would have tried to explain them. Let those of knowledge keep in view this aspect of these verses.

Verses 17-18:
Further warning:
Within the word "wa'z" (advice/admonition), the meaning of rebuke and prohibition is itself implied; for this reason, there is no need to bring the letter "La" after it. These verses too are by way of warning, in the manner three verses have passed above. The directions given in this Surah are highly necessary for remaining safe from the anger and wrath of God; for this reason, after short intervals, these warnings are coming repeatedly. The meaning is: "God is warning you that such an action should never be committed by you again. 'In kuntum mu'mineen'—meaning, avoiding such things is the inevitable requirement of faith. If they are not avoided, the claim of faith will remain completely meaningless. 'Wa yubayyinu Allahu...'—meaning, Allah has clearly explained His directions; now the responsibility is yours, and remember: Allah is All-Knowing and All-Wise, because of which what He has told contains all good and blessing for you." The meaning is: these commands are told and taught by an All-Knowing and All-Wise; no one should fall into the delusion of being more knowing and wise than God.

The conspiracy of the Jews in raising the trial:
This points to those hypocrites who raised this trial. They were the allies of the Jews, and it was highly heavy upon the Jews that the awe of the Muslims' moral superiority was settling also on the hearts of their opponents. The society of the Arabs in the era of ignorance had become completely a society of the wicked, and the state of the Jews, despite being People of the Book, was even worse than theirs. Now, when they saw that from inside this very heap of filth, a pure society of the righteous was emerging which was worthy of envy even for the angels, they saw their own image very ugly in this mirror. To take revenge for this, the plan they thought of was: the spreading of evil and indecency should occur among the Muslims too, and for this campaign, they made their allied and nurtured hypocrites the medium, who targeted specifically the Household of the Mission, so that if they succeeded in this conspiracy of theirs, the entire building of the Muslims' moral prestige would collapse. But Allah, by His favor and mercy, did not let their conspiracy succeed... Pointing to these wicked ones, He said: "Indeed, those who love that indecency should spread among the believers—for them is a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter."

"Wallahu ya'lamu wa antum la ta'lamoon" (And Allah knows, while you do not know)—this is a warning to those Muslims who, because of not being well-acquainted with these hidden corrupt ones, fell into the wave of the trials raised by them, and on this occasion too, some fell.

Severe warning:
The exact same verse has passed above. See verse 10. The only difference is: there it was Accepting of Repentance and Wise, and here it is Kind and Merciful. The reply to the condition (jawab ash-shart) is omitted here too, and this omission is proof of the intensity of the warning. The meaning is: this is the favor of Allah and His mercy that He did not grasp you (address to the Muslims) on this severe mistake of yours, otherwise, the manner in which the people inside you had provided the means for the enemies of Islam, you were worthy of falling into the grasp of God; but God is Kind and Merciful, because of which He, despite your mistake, gave you the respite of repentance and reform.

This is a warning to the Muslims, of the same kind as the warnings that have passed above. He said: "Do not follow the footsteps of Satan and his agents. Remember this well: whenever Satan advises, he will advise only the path of evil and indecency; he will never advise the path of virtue and goodness." This warning is indeed general, but here specifically, that direction which passed above has been confirmed: in the matter of anyone's honor and respect, do not relate every word regarding which you have no knowledge. The statement for which there is no proof is mere assumption, and following assumption is following Satan.

A subtle and delicate warning:
"Wa lawla fadlu Allahi 'alaykum wa rahmatuhu ma zaka minkum min ahadin abadan walakin Allaha yuzakkee man yasha'u"—this is a highly subtle and delicate warning within this connection: those who accept every kind of statement easily in the matter of others' honor and respect, and fall into bad-thinking regarding them, their attitude reflects that they are involved in an excessive good-thinking regarding themselves, rather in a kind of claim of purification, whether they possess the consciousness of this inner thief of theirs or not. From this hidden thief, He has warned those here who accepted the rumor spread by the enemies regarding the Mother of the Believers without any rejection. He said: "Let everyone remember: whoever obtains any purity, obtains it merely by the favor of Allah. If His support is not conjoined, no one can become pure—so no one should be proud of their piety and purification, to accept every kind of statement without investigation in the matter of others."

Within "Wallahu samee'un 'aleemun" (And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing), there is the reminder: the person who is involved in excessive good-thinking regarding himself must not forget that God is All-Hearing and All-Knowing. He hears and knows the most hidden thing of every person.

Direction to pardon those who fell into this trial merely out of simplicity:
Now, conjoined to the above matter, direction is given of pardon and forgiveness in the case of those who fell into the relation and transmission of this trial merely out of their simplicity, failing to estimate its severity. The need for this direction was because after the actual reality became clear, there was fear: many people would withdraw their support and patronage from those who had, in some capacity, taken part in spreading this lie. In the narrations, it is: Mistah, a poor companion, was the relative of Hazrat Abu Bakr. Hazrat Abu Bakr used to patronize him; when you came to know that he too was a partner in this trial, naturally you felt severe grief, and you swore an oath: "Now, I will never help Mistah." This kind of attitude was feared from other zealous Muslims too; for this reason, it was directed: those whom Allah has favored with His grace and granted expansion of provision and wealth should not swear oaths to deprive close relatives, the needy, and those who migrated in the path of Allah of their patronage and help because of some mistake of theirs; rather, let them work with pardon and forgiveness—mistakes and shortcomings occur from everyone, but Allah pardons. If He were to grasp on everyone's mistake and deprive them of His favors, who could escape His grasp! So, those who are seekers of Allah's forgiveness for their own mistakes must also forgive the mistakes of others. Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. He wants the reflection of His attributes to be inside His servants too.

In the verse, the words "ooloo al-fadli minkum was-sa'ati" (those of favor among you and expansion) are to remind of the right and responsibility: those whom Allah favors with His grace and expansion of provision—He wants from them: in the manner God has given them with expansion despite their shortcomings and mistakes, similarly they too should give to His servants with generosity and expansion, and not withdraw their hand of help on being affected by some mistake of theirs.

The words "oolee al-qurba wal-masakeena wal-muhajireena fee sabeeli Allahi" are for expressing the entitlement: those who are close relatives too, needy too, and who have borne the difficulties of migration in the path of Allah, after all, possess a right, and they will not be deprived of their right merely on the basis that they committed a mistake without realizing it.

Within "Ala tuhibboona an yaghfira Allahu lakum" (Do you not love that Allah should forgive you?), there is the pointer to that delicate reality to which pointer is made in the above verse: just as falling into bad-thinking regarding someone merely on the basis of rumors is proof of excessive good-thinking regarding oneself, rather of the claim of purification, similarly not pardoning someone's mistake and depriving him of one's patronage is equivalent to considering oneself free of mistakes, although a person may not possess the consciousness of this hidden thought of his. Pondering from this aspect, it becomes clear: from the perspective of the wisdom of religion, both these mistakes belong to the same family. For this reason, the mention of both together was appropriate.

Verses 23-25:
The severity of the hypocrites' crime and a severe warning to them:
"Muhsanat" (chaste women) here is in the meaning of decent and free women, and by "ghafilat" (unaware/innocent) is meant those decent women who are unaware of the deceptions and deceptions of the world. Although the words are general, and by them are meant all decent women of the Muslim society of the time, within them is a subtle pointer specifically to Hazrat Aisha as-Siddiqah—she is the highest subject of these words.

"Ramy" originally means throwing an arrow, but it is used also in the meaning of accusing and slandering someone. Here, in the use of this word for accusing the "ghafilat", there is a specific eloquence. Within it is the metaphor: those who accuse decent and innocent women are as if they are targeting them with their arrows while they are asleep. He said: "On such people is the curse of God in both this world and the Hereafter. Neither will they flourish in this world, nor in the Hereafter; in this world too, very soon, their root will be cut, and in the Hereafter, there is indeed a great punishment for them." The effect of this curse of Allah upon these hypocrites ultimately manifested: the dominance of Islam wiped out their and their leaders' existence from the land of Arabia.

"Yawma tashhadu 'alayhim alsinatuhum..."—meaning, on the day they deal with the punishment of God, on that day, neither will they be able to present any excuse, nor will there be need for any external testimony to prove their crime. On that day, their tongues, their hands, and their feet themselves will testify against them. Today, they can put a veil on these hidden conspiracies and rishedawanees of theirs, but from their tongues and their hands and feet, no movement of theirs is hidden. From these very witnesses, God will make them testify as to what slanders they fabricated and what evil and corruption they raised.

"Yawma'idhin yuwaffeehim Allahu deenahum al-haqqa"—"deen" here is in the meaning of reward and jaza. That is: on that day, Allah will pay their due reward fully. There will be neither any favor for them, nor any injustice.

"Wa ya'lamoona anna Allaha huwa al-haqqu al-mubeenu"—meaning, today, since a veil is still on the situations, for this reason, they are involved in many misunderstandings regarding Allah; neither is there the certainty that Allah is entirely truth and justice, and for this reason, He will inevitably make the truth dominant and destroy falsehood; nor is there the certainty that He is "Al-Mubeen"—meaning, He who clarifies all realities. These misunderstandings have made them very clever in their mischiefs, but on that day, this reality will be clearly illuminated upon them: God is entirely truth and justice, and along with it, He is "Al-Mubeen"—the Clarifier of all realities.

The classification of souls in the Hereafter will be according to morals:
That is, in this world indeed, a veil is still on the situations, and both pure and impure are mixed together, but when the Day of True Reward comes, on that day, the classification of souls, as He said in "Wa-idha an-nufusu zuwwijat" [At-Takwir: 7], will be according to deeds, morals, and faith. On that day, evil women will be paired with evil men, and evil men with evil women. In contrast to it, decent-moral women will obtain the companionship of decent-moral men, and decent-moral men will find decent-moral wives, and these people, on that day, will be completely free and pure from all those absurdities and talks being said against them, and for them will be forgiveness and noble provision... We have explained in another place: these two words come as a comprehensive expression for all blessings of the Hereafter.

The upcoming theme... Verses 27-34:
Now forward, He has closed those rungs which lead to bad-sightedness, accusation, and moral corruption in society. In this connection, first of all, He has imposed some restrictions on that freedom which the people of the society of ignorance possessed regarding coming and going in others' houses. Then, He has directed the marriage of the single and the capable slave-girls and slaves, so that the wicked may find no way for creating mischief in society. Within this, He opened a highly spacious path for the freedom of slaves through "mukathabah" (contract of freedom), and urged people to help them in acquiring this freedom, so that the slaves might enter the ranks of the free, their moral standard might be raised, and a perfect harmony and uniformity might be born in society on the basis of faith... In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

Direction for seeking permission to enter houses:
"Isti'nas" means to become acquainted and familiar, and by "buyoot" (houses) is meant, as explained in the subsequent verse, residential houses in which anyone's wife and children live. He said: "If any Muslim has need to go to his other Muslim brother's residential house, he must not enter directly, but first acquire familiarity and acquaintance, whose civilized and blessed method is: stand at the door and offer greeting (salam) to the household. If reply to the greeting and permission are received from there, enter the house, otherwise return."

Greeting is a highly pure and clean method of introducing oneself and seeking permission. In the verse, "tas-ta'nisoo" is for explaining the method of acquaintance, followed by "wa tusallimoo". It is obvious: when someone offers greeting at the door, the household will find out who he is, what he wants, and whether his entering the house is appropriate or not, and whether his coming at this time is appropriate or at some other time.

The method of greeting for permission:
The method of seeking permission to enter houses has always been present in some form in all civilized nations. The nobles of the ignorance of the Arabs also had this practice; rather, the words they had for it were almost of the same meaning as are currently in force in Western or Westernized society, but Islam did not adopt them; instead, He directed greeting in their place, and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) showed this method of greeting: stand at the door and offer greeting, if some reply is received, excellent; and if no reply is received, after offering greeting up to three times, return.

After this restriction, it is obvious: the possibilities of this danger were stopped that some unwanted person might enter anyone's female apartment at some inappropriate time. After this, only those people could enter houses who either possess some relationship with the household or whom the household trusts completely.

The blessings of this method:
"Dhalikum khayrun lakum la'allakum tadhakkaroon"—this has explained the blessing of this restriction so that it may not be highly hard on natures. He said: "This is a cause of good and blessing for you, so that you may obtain remembrance." As if its nature is like a blessed notice at the door: that the one entering the house also obtains the remembrance that he is stepping into another's sanctuary, where he has to consider some specific moral manners, and the household also obtains the information that there is now a person inside whose case some limits and conditions are upon them, whose respect is a cause of good and blessing in both this world and the Hereafter.

That is, if after seeking permission, it is known: there is no one in the house to give permission, do not enter the house until permission is received; and if you are told to return, then return.

"Huwa azka lakum"—meaning: "Although this is hard on the heart, from the moral perspective, this method alone is pure." It is not known what situations are there because of which the household did not give permission. In such matters, considering the interests of the household more than one's own respect is wisdom and security from the trials of Satan.

The wisdom of the repeated reminder of God's attribute of knowledge:
"Wallahu bima ta'maloona 'aleem"—this theme, with minor changes in words, has entered repeatedly here. Forward, He said: (And Allah knows what you reveal and what you conceal) [An-Noor: 29]. Then, it was said: (Indeed, Allah is Acquainted with what they do) [An-Noor: 30]. This repeated reminder of God's attribute of knowledge is because: the things which are prohibited here have many backdoor-doors inside the soul. Unless the correct recall of God's attribute of knowledge is present, it is not possible to close these rungs with mere commands and directions. Satan finds some way, and man stumbles. However, if this thought dominates the heart at all times that Allah is observing everything, this thing protects man even from the most hidden tricks of Satan.

By "ghayra maskoonatin" (non-residential), context is proof that those houses are meant here which are non-residential—meaning, in which no one's wife and children live. From the above directions, it is clear: this restriction of seeking permission was imposed principally to protect from bad-sightedness and accusation; for this reason, this restriction is not necessary for those houses which are non-residential. In them, a person can enter without permission, but along with it, the restriction "feeha mata'un lakum" (in which is utility for you) is conjoined. That is: some utility and need is the motivator for him. From "mata'un lakum", the point emerges: they must be houses of common utility—for example, shops, inns, hotels, etc.; male sitting-rooms also enter in this command.

"Wallahu ya'lamu ma tubdoona wa ma taktumoon"—this is a warning of the same kind as the warnings that have passed above. This warning was necessary because: if a person does not have the correct recall of God's attribute of knowledge inside himself, he can take wrong advantage even of this freedom. A person may enter anyone's female apartment without permission, and when criticized, make this excuse: "I assumed this to be a male sitting-room or office."

Verses 30-31:
Cautions after being inside the house:
After the aforementioned permission, if a person enters anyone's house, he is directed: let him lower his gaze, and guard his private parts. From "qul lil-mu'mineena" (say to the believers), the point emerges: this lowering of the gaze and guarding of private parts is the requirement of faith. And from "min absarihim" (of their gaze), the point emerges: the purpose is to keep gazes lowered to some extent—meaning, to keep his gaze protected from the women of the house, because this direction, in reality, is given keeping them in view. From this, this point also emerged: the permission Islam has given is given only for the purpose of removing hardship for the relatives and trusted ones of the household. Particularly because: every person's house in that era did not have a male sitting-room, nor can it have today; but these directions themselves are proof: if the arrangement for men to sit separately is present in the house, the sitting of men other than close relatives separately from the women of the house is desired in Islam.

Importance of the direction of lowering the gaze:
The direction of lowering the gaze is a highly important direction—the gaze, in reality, serves as the primary messenger between man and woman. If anyone places a guard over it with honesty, he is protected from many of the trials of Satan.

To the guarding of private parts, the primary part is held by clothing:
To the guarding of private parts, a great part is held by clothing being decent. In this era, due to the desire for fashion, such inventions have been made in clothing that its actual purpose—covering (satr)—has completely disappeared. Only the purpose of decoration has remained, and that decoration too in which open bad-proportionedness is prominent. Formerly, there was only the weeping over women, but now men too have adopted such clothing that in this clothing, they have no right to enter any decent assembly of the people, let alone entering any believing Muslim's house.

"Dhalika azka lahum inna Allaha khabeerun bima yasna'oon"—meaning, these cautions inside houses are highly noble and important from the perspective of moral purity, on the condition: along with these cautions, this reality also remains always recalled that whatever man does, no matter how hiddenly he does it, God is aware of it. If this reality is not recalled, neither can the gaze be protected from wildness, nor the organs from display.

"Wa qul lil-mu'minati yaghdudna min absarihinna wa yahfadna furoojahunna wa la yubdeena zeenatahunna illa ma dhahara minha wal-yadribna bikhumurihinna 'ala juyoobihinna wa la yubdeena zeenatahunna illa libu'oolatihinna aw aba'ihinna aw aba'i bu'oolatihinna aw abna'ihinna aw abna'i bu'oolatihinna aw ikhwanihinna aw banee ikhwanihinna aw banee akhawatihinna aw nisa'ihinna aw ma malakat aymanuhunna awi at-tabi'eena ghayri oolee al-irbati mina ar-rijali awi at-tifli alladhina lam yadhharoo 'ala 'awrati an-nisa'i wa la yadribna bi-arjulihinna liyu'lama ma yukhfeena min zeenatihinn. Wa tooboo ila Allahi jamee'an ayyuha al-mu'minoona la'allakum tuflihoon."

The veil of women inside the house:
Now, women are being directed: if any non-relative is present inside the house, they too must lower their gazes, guard their private parts, and must not reveal their decoration before him; only that decoration is exempt from this whose being revealed is unavoidable. The revealing of the decoration is permissible before the husband, or before the close relatives (mahrams), or before the women of their association and service, or before their clerks, or before those men who have become old and the burden of whose care has been assumed by the household, or before those children who are still unacquainted with sexual matters; and furthermore: if women need to move inside the house in the presence of any non-relative, they must not walk striking their feet on the ground so that the jingling of ornaments is heard.

This is the simple meaning of this verse. Some of its parts which require explanation, we explain them too.

Meaning of "Illa ma dhahara minha":
Within "wa la yubdeena zeenatahunna illa ma dhahara minha" (and let them not reveal their decoration except what is apparent of it), inside decoration, both clothing and ornaments are included. Neither is it possible to hide every single thing of them, nor is the being revealed of every thing unavoidable. The apparent part of the clothing will inevitably remain revealed, and ornaments too, particularly some ornaments of the hand, cannot be hidden without hardship. By the exception "illa ma dhahara minha", saving from this kind of extraordinary hardship, He directed to hide only those decorations in whose hiding there is not much hardship.

The head-covering is a necessary part of Muslim women's clothing:
"Wal-yadribna bikhumurihinna 'ala juyoobihinna"—"khimar" means dupatta and head-covering. From this, one thing known is: the head-covering is a necessary part of the clothing of Muslim women. The second thing known is: in the presence of any non-relative, it is necessary for women to cover their heads and backs along with their bosoms with this head-covering... On one hand, keep this command in view, and on the other hand, see the displayers (mutabarrijat) of our nation—that they, declaring the head-covering as old-fashionedness, excluded it entirely, and made the cleavage of the chest and the bosom's rise as prominent as possible, establishing this as a requirement of civilization!

"Aw aba'ihinna"—the word "aba'" (fathers) in its wide meaning is inclusive of both grandfathers and uncles. The names of the prominent mahram relatives are counted, but meant are all those who enter in this command.

Meaning of "Nisa'ihinn":
In "aw nisa'ihinna" (or their women), some jurists have meant only Muslim women, but this is not correct. In our view, the opinion of the author of 'Kashshaf' is correct in this. He has meant the women of association, company, and service (man fee suhbatihinna wa khidmatihinna mina an-nisa'). It should be kept in mind: revealing one's decoration before any kind of strange women is not free of trial and danger for women. Some women are highly wicked. No one knows what calamity they may put one in; remaining safe from them is security.

"Ma malakat aymanuhunna" includes both slave-girls and slaves:
"Aw ma malakat aymanuhunna" includes both slave-girls and slaves. Some jurists have meant only slave-girls, but no clue of this specificity is present in the words. If only slave-girls were meant, the correct and clear expression would have been "aw ima'ihinn"; a general word, which is inclusive of both slave-girls and slaves, would not have been used for it. Furthermore, before this, the word "nisa'ihinn" has entered, which, as has been explained, is inclusive of all those women who possess the association and service relationship; after this, there remains no need for a separate mention of slave-girls. Additionally, this is also contrary to the specifications of the Quran. Forward in this same Surah, some explanations regarding these commands will come, which were sent down later. In them, both slave-girls and slaves are permitted to enter and exit the house without permission, except during three specific times, and the reason for this has been explained: (they are constant visitors among you, some of you around others) [An-Noor: 58]. [1] For these reasons, I consider the opinion of those jurists correct who believe this word is general for both slave-girls and slaves. However, for them too, that restriction will be necessary which the Quran itself has explained, and whose explanation will come forward in verse 58. But the nature of this discussion is now merely of an academic discussion, because the issue of slavery has now been finished, and in this connection, in the history of the world, first of all, Islam took steps, whose details are coming forward. Now, the issue is not of slaves, but of servants and employees. Regarding them, the point emerging from the indications of the Quran is: as far as old employees and children are concerned, except during three times whose details are coming, there is no restriction on their entering and exiting. For young employees, in my view, it is necessary to act on all those directions which were mentioned above. For them, seeking permission before entering the house is also necessary, and in their presence in the house, those restrictions are also obligatory upon them and the women of the house which are mentioned in the verses under discussion.

The command of old men and the eunuchs:
By "awi at-tabi'eena ghayri oolee al-irbati mina ar-rijali", those old, disabled, blind, and lame men are meant who have gone out of the era of sexual desires and yearns. If the care and patronage of any of such men is assumed by the household and they live there, then in their case too, those restrictions are not there which are mentioned above. In my view, this same is the command of old servants, only during those three times is the permission necessary for them which is mentioned forward in verse 58.

The command of children:
By "awi at-tifli alladhina lam yadhharoo 'ala 'awrati an-nisa'i", those children are meant in whom sexual consciousness has not yet awakened, and they do not look at the private parts of women with that gaze with which a man looks. For them, at first, the restriction of seeking permission was not there, but later, during three times, the restriction was imposed on them too. The detail of this will come forward.

The display of decoration has a specific circle:
Within "wa la yadribna bi-arjulihinna liyu'lama ma yukhfeena min zeenatihinna" (and let them not strike their feet to make known what they conceal of their decoration), in the use of decoration, the primary feeling is of display. Although this thing is inside the nature of man, particularly woman, it has a specific circle. Like the displayers of the present era, displaying one's decoration everywhere is not permissible in Islam. For this reason, this direction was given: in the presence of non-relatives, if women need to walk inside the house, they must not walk striking their feet on the ground so that the jingling of ornaments is heard.

Direction of collective repentance for Muslims:
"Wa tooboo ila Allahi jamee'an ayyuha al-mu'minoona la'allakum tuflihoon"—since these directions have been given for the reform and purification of the entire Muslim society, addressing all, the invitation to turn to Allah was given: all of you together, reform your mistakes and shortcomings of past, and adopt that way of life to which the Quran is guiding you; this is the path of success. The conditions of this success have been explained under Surah Al-Hajj, verse 77, and Surah Al-Mu'minoon, verse 1. If need for detail is there, take a look at them.

The commands of the veil outside houses are explained in Surah Al-Ahzab:
In both the above verses, directions have been given to both men and women; the context and words of the verses make it clear: these relate to the veil inside houses. As for the question: if any woman needs to step out of the house for some necessity, what must she do in this case? This question's answer is not given here. Its answer will come in Surah Al-Ahzab. If God wills, we will discuss it in detail there. Anyway, we have also written a separate treatise under the title 'The Quran and the Veil', in which we have clarified in the light of the Quran: for both inside and outside situations, the Quran has given highly decisive commands of the veil. Those seeking details may read that treatise. Even after these clear commands, those who do not see the command of the veil anywhere in the Quran—what can be said regarding them except that the lowering of the gaze the Quran directed from non-relative women, these gentlemen have done from the Quran itself! These people kiss the Quran with great devotion, but with closed eyes, and after kissing, leave it, considering it a heavy stone!

Verses 32-33:
"Ayama" is the plural of "ayyim". "Ayyim" refers to both that man who is deprived of a wife and that woman who is deprived of a husband—meaning, both widows/widowers and divorcees enter in this word; rather, in its wide meaning, it is used also for those men and women who have remained unmarried.

Meaning of "Salihin":
The word "salihin" (righteous) is used here in a comprehensive meaning for those possessing both physical and moral capabilities. In Arabic, this word is used in this comprehensive meaning.

Direction for the marriage of the unmarried:
Inside the society, a wide door for moral corruptions and the intrusions of Satan is also opened by this: many men are deprived of wives and many women are deprived of husbands. From this, the possibilities of unlawful relations increase greatly, and for the wicked, opportunities also appear to spread slanders. In the birth of this evil, just as in some situations poverty has a part, similarly in some situations, particularly in the case of women, the customs and rituals of families, brotherhoods, and tribes also have a great part. In some families and brotherhoods, the marriage of widows is considered defective. It is obvious: the reform of such evils cannot happen until the feeling of their reform is born inside society itself. For this reason, the Quran directed society: marry the single among you and those who are unmarried among your men and women. The meaning is: create a feeling inside people for this thing, eradicate its opposing trends, encourage and help those who are poor, so that they too may obtain their lawful place inside society, and the harms which can reach them and society due to their remaining unmarried may be stopped.

Similarly, marry those among your slaves and slave-girls who are capable in terms of physical and moral capabilities. By capable is meant those slave-girls and slaves who can bear their responsibilities and understand their rights and duties. This direction was also given because: in this manner, the moral and social standard of the slaves will be raised, and they will gradually become righteous parts of society.

Marriage increases provision and favor:
"In yakoonoo fuqara'a yughnihimu Allahu min fadlihi" (If they should be poor, Allah will enrich them from His bounty)—above we have pointed out: the common cause for men remaining deprived of marriage is poverty. A poor man, first of all, himself hesitates to bear the responsibility of a family; but even if he does not hesitate, what woman is ready to give herself into his marriage-contract! To remove this obstacle, Allah gave this glad tiding: those who are poor, let them remain assured, and let others also remain assured, that marriage does not increase poverty but increases God's provision and favor. The person who marries to protect his faith and morals is under the eye of mercy of Allah, and He supports him. As long as a person remains deprived of a wife, he remains somewhat nomadic, and many of his capabilities remain contracted and suppressed. Similarly, as long as a woman remains deprived of a husband, her status too is as of a vine which, due to not finding support, is deprived of spreading, blooming, and bearing fruit. But when the woman finds a husband and the man obtains the company of a wife, the capabilities of both emerge, and when they both struggle together in the field of life, Allah gives blessing in their struggle, and their situations change completely.

"Wallahu wasi'un 'aleemun" (And Allah is all-Encompassing and Knowing)—to confirm this glad tiding, Allah has referenced His attributes: that in the mantle of God's favor is great capacity, and He is well-aware of everyone's situations and needs.

"Walyasta'fifi alladhina la yajidoona nikahan hatta yughniyahumu Allahu min fadlihi walladhina yabtaghoona al-kitaba mimma malakat aymanukum fakatiboohum in 'alimtum feehim khayran wa-atoohum min mali Allahi alladhee atakum wa-la tukrihoo fatayatikum 'ala al-bigha'i in aradna tahassunan litabtaghoo 'arada al-hayati ad-dunya wa-man yukrihhunna fa-inna Allaha min ba'di ikrahihinna ghafoorun raheemun."

The lack of capability for marriage is no excuse for bad deeds:
"Walyasta'fifi alladhina la yajidoona nikahan"—the above direction was for society, but the moral responsibility of individuals remains under all circumstances. If some person is not finding the capability for marriage, this is no excuse under any circumstances for the permissibility of bad deeds. For such a person, it is necessary to keep himself in control and wait for the favor and mercy of God. The person who wages this Jihad to protect his faith and morals—Allah will open the path for him. From the words of the verse, this point emerges.

Meaning of "Mukathabah" (contract of freedom) and its command:
"Walladhina yabtaghoona al-kitaba mimma malakat aymanukum..." until the end of the verse. "Kitab" and "mukathabah" is a terminology whose meaning is: some slave desires from his master that he should make him a contract—that within such period, he will pay him such amount or perform some specific service for him, after which the master will free him. In such a case, the command is: make him a contract of freedom. Common scholars indeed take this command in the meaning of recommendation and desirability, but Hazrat Umar, Ibn Sirin, and Dawud have taken it in the meaning of obligation. In their view, making him a contract of freedom is obligatory upon the master; in my view, this school is strong. These commands, as pointed out above, are being given to society; for this reason, in this case, the master's status does not remain as an absolute independent master, but becomes of a party. If both the slave and the master make the transaction between themselves, excellent; and if any dispute occurs between them, the responsible people of society will decide it, and as a last resort, if need arises, the matter can also go to court.

Capability for "Mukathabah":
"In 'alimtum feehim khayran"—in this case, only this thing will be seen: whether the slave demanding the contract of freedom possesses such capability of work and such sense of responsibility or not, to fulfill the contract made with the master. The need for this condition was because: there was also the possibility that some lazy slave, to obtain freedom from the master, might demand the contract of freedom, but possess no capability of work, and make this thing merely a excuse for asking help from the public and adopting the profession of begging, remaining as a burden on society. The cause for this anxiety was: after some slave became contracted for freedom, regarding him, the Quran directed people, as is coming forward, to help him with open hearts, and help be given to him from the treasury (Bayt al-Mal) too, so that he might become capable of paying the amount of his contract. It is obvious: the greed of this thing could arouse the yearn of becoming contracted even inside those slaves who themselves were not capable of shifting their hands and feet, but on being freed from the master's service, they would become a burden on society, and if they could not fulfill the conditions of the contract, they would inevitably be a medium of dispute and difference.

But it should be kept in mind: this condition could not be an obstacle to any capable slave's freedom. This is because in this also, in case of dispute, the final decision was in the choice of society and the judge (qadi). If any slave had the complaint: his master, making this condition an excuse, wants to place obstacles in his freedom, while he possesses the full capability to become contracted, he could present his matter before the responsible people and arbiters of society, and as a last resort, take it to court too.

Helping the contracted is spending in the path of Allah:
"Wa-atoohum min mali Allahi alladhee atakum"—this has urged society: those slaves who gird up their loins for their freedom—of your wealth which God has given you, with full generosity, help them. This encouragement is given to the wealthy, and the words "min mali Allahi alladhee atakum" (of the wealth of Allah which He gave you) are to arouse the feeling of gratitude inside them: do not hesitate to spend in God's path what is God's giving. From this, this point also emerged: those who spend for this purpose—their spending will be in the path of Allah.

Helping the contracted from Zakah funds:
It should be kept in mind: the predecessors are agreed on this—such slaves must be helped from Zakah and the treasury too. In the treasury, for the freedom of slaves, Islam has kept an independent fund, whose explanation has passed in Surah At-Tawbah.

This right of contract of freedom, in the manner Islam gave it to slaves, gave it to slave-girls too. The generality of the words "mimma malakat aymanukum" is also proof of this, and the action of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the companions too. In the narrations, the events of Hazrat Barirah and Hazrat Juwayriyah are present.

After this law was sent down, the highly spacious path for the freedom of all capable slaves and slave-girls opened. After this, only those slaves remained who either were content and pleased with their state, or did not possess the capability to shift their hands and feet and create their own place inside society. It is obvious: if the general declaration of freedom of such slaves and slave-girls had been made, its result would have been: they would have been deprived of their masters' patronage too, and would also not have been able to make the arrangement for their own living, because of which they would have remained as a burden on society.

"Wa-la tukrihoo fatayatikum 'ala al-bigha'i in aradna tahassunan litabtaghoo 'arada al-hayati ad-dunya..."—"fatayat" (girls/young women) is used here for slave-girls, and the purpose of using this word is to increase their respect inside society. "Fatayah" is the feminine of "fata"; just as "fata" means young and boy, similarly "fatayah" means girl. In the Hadith, it is: the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) said: let no one call his slave "'abd" (slave) and his slave-girl "amah" (slave-girl); rather, let him say "fata" (young man) and "fatayah" (girl). It is obvious: this change of words for slave-girls and slaves is also a part of those reforms which appeared to raise their social status, as mentioned above. The purpose of this change of words was to awaken the sense of self-respect inside slaves and slave-girls, and to direct people to this reality: now people should change their ancient conceptions regarding slaves and slave-girls, and look at them with the eye of brothers and sisters of their society.

The restriction "in aradna tahassunan" (if they should desire chastity) is here not by way of condition, but for the expression of the state of the occurrence. When Islam gave the command to execute limits on fornication, directed the marriage of slaves and slave-girls, and opened a highly spacious path for the freedom of slave-girls and slaves through contract of freedom, naturally, a general feeling was awakened inside slave-girls too: they should raise their moral standard, and those among them who, because of the pressure of their masters, did prostitution, desired: they should leave this unlawful profession to lead a chaste life, and if any form appeared, marry someone to lead a life. In this era of reforms, the birth of this trend was a natural thing, but their blow fell on the interest of those masters who had established brothels in the era of ignorance, and who made their slave-girls do prostitution to benefit from their income. Some of such wicked people, even in the era of Islam, as we have pointed out, were running brothels secretly. It is present in history: the famous hypocrite Abdullah bin Ubayy had established a brothel. It is obvious: such wicked people could not easily be pleased with letting these slave-girls go out of their clutches. Consequently, they used all their weapons to stop them from leading a chaste life. Pointing to those people, by way of warning, He said: "Now, when these girls want to choose a chaste life, do not force them to prostitution. Otherwise remember: in the case of coercion, Allah indeed will forgive them; however, let those people think of their end who are playing this disgusting game with their chastity!" Remember here that thing to which we have pointed: some of the brothel-owners were also stoned by the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in the later stages.

The material for advice:
This is a warning of the same kind as the warnings that have entered repeatedly in this Surah. Parallel examples of it have passed before, and forward in verse 46, its parallel is also present. On the wisdom of the repeated representation of these warnings, we have pointed under the first verse of this Surah. "Wa mathalan mina alladhina khalaw min qablikum" points to that parable which is coming forward in verse 35, and whose explanation will come before you. The meaning is: We have sent down these highly clear directions to you, and for your encouragement, have also presented the parable of those righteous predecessors whose footsteps you must follow. In them, there is great advice and admonition for those who fear God. As for those whose breasts are empty of the fear of God—no clarity and advice is effective upon them. They will see their end themselves!

[6] The gradual eradication of slavery:
In this group of verses, the important directions given have been explained under the verses according to need. However, regarding slavery, we want to combine here those matters to which we have pointed in places, so that those misunderstandings present in the minds of people in this connection may be eradicated.

Slavery was no part of Islam's own system; rather, Islam merely tolerated it because of the international and domestic situations of the time, knowing: if by a single stroke of the pen, the announcement of its end had been made, this would have strengthened the enemy nations and would have created highly severe social and economic entanglements inside its own society. In that era, first: a well-known solution for the issue of prisoners of war was only this: those captured in war, the victors made them slaves; second: everywhere, on the slave-markets, the transaction of slaves and slave-girls was active, and in every wealthy house, slave-girls and slaves of every age were present. In such situations, if at once the announcement had been made that all slave-girls and slaves are free, regardless of whether the opposing powers would have benefited from it, a highly important question would have arisen: where should all these slave-girls and slaves be accommodated in society? Among them, a very large number was of those individuals who did not possess the capability to bear the burden of their own living. In the state of slavery, the responsibility for their living was legally upon their master; but in the case of becoming free, there remained no other way for them except to beg and remain as a burden on society. Particularly, the issue of slave-girls was even more complex. In their becoming helpless, many moral corruptions were feared. The treasury (Bayt al-Mal), as is obvious, could not bear the burden of the living of such a large army. Because of these interests, Islam adopted such a path for their freedom from which those dangers did not arise, and gradually all capable slaves and slave-girls became free to become parts of the Muslim society.

Here, there is no room to discuss all the details of this issue. For it, an independent book is required. However, we will point to some important principles from which you can estimate what directions Islam issued gradually to bring slaves into the ranks of the free.

1. Islam took the first step in this connection: from the very early period of Makkan life, "fakk raqabah"—meaning freeing a slave—was established as a very great social virtue. In Makkan surahs, "fakk raqabah" and feeding the poor are mentioned together, and in some verses, this is stated as the first step on the path of attaining success, particularly for the wealthy.
2. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in highly clear words directed not only good behavior with slaves and slave-girls, but also made those human rights of theirs a legal status, because of which the masters' absolute choice and power over them which was in the era of ignorance was finished.
3. In the case of some sins and shortcomings, freeing a slave was made the expiation (kaffarah) and charity.
4. Direction was given to marry all capable slave-girls and slaves, so that their moral and social standard might be raised.
5. Among the expenditures of Zakah, an independent fund "fe-ar-riqab" (for freeing slaves) was kept, to strengthen the campaign of freeing slaves.
6. Brothels which the masters had established in the era of ignorance to make their slave-girls do prostitution were finished after the crime of fornication was established, and if anyone tried to run this business secretly, he was given highly exemplary punishment. To the extent that some of such people were stoned by the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).
7. Instead of using the contemptuous words "'abd" and "amah" for slaves and slave-girls, directions were given to use "fata" and "fatayah", to change people's ancient conceptions regarding this class.
8. Generally, regarding prisoners of war, this policy was adopted: the prominent leaders indeed were killed, but the common prisoners were freed after receiving ransom or out of favor.
9. Finally, "mukathabah" (contract of freedom) was given a legal status, after which a highly spacious path for the freedom of every capable slave and slave-girl opened. For their help and encouragement, common people were also urged, and for them, a fund was kept in the treasury too. This law practically ended slavery. After this, only those slaves remained who were devoid of the capability to shift their hands and feet, and for them, this state was better both for society and themselves, otherwise they would have been a burden on society and forced to adopt the profession of begging.

The proofs of all these matters are present in detail in the Quran and Hadith. I can present them, but the discussion will exceed its limits; therefore, I content myself with pointers. In the previous discussions, the proofs of most of them have been explained, and if the proof of any matter has not been explained, in the light of these pointers, those of knowledge can easily provide their proofs.

The upcoming theme... Verses 35-40:
Now forward, those verses are coming whose status inside this Surah is as of a brilliant sun, whose light is falling on all parts of the Surah. In them, the parables of both faith and disbelief are described, and it is shown what light is born within the apparent and hidden of man through faith, and how from it, actions are born in individual and social life; and what kind of darkness is cast on the apparent and hidden of man through disbelief, and ultimately how it imposes destruction on individual and social life. The purpose of presenting these parables is to clarify to the believers that they should illuminate every sphere of their life with the light of faith, and to clarify to the disbelievers that if they want to remain in that very darkness of disbelief, one above another, and do not want to come into the light of faith, let them think of their end well!... In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

For one who does not believe in God, this universe is a world of darkness:
"Allahu nooru as-samawati wal-ardi" (Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth)—this heaven and earth, rather this entire universe, is a world of darkness and a place of confusion for that person who does not believe in God, or believes but does not believe in His attributes and their demands. Such a person can neither know from where this world came, nor can know what the purpose of its coming into existence is. He cannot decide regarding himself too: what is the purpose of his existence? Is he unbridled in this world, or bound and subject? Is he accountable or not? What is good for him, and what is evil? Should he adopt the path of wrong, or of justice? Should he follow only his interest and desires, or some higher goal? The correct answer to these questions is necessary for a correct and successful life, but the person who does not believe in God cannot find the correct solution to these questions. He wanders like a blind bull.

For one who believes in God, his night too is as bright as day:
And ultimately, one day, he falls into the palace of destruction to meet his end. However, the person who believes in God with His correct attributes gets the key of this universe, and its end also becomes clear to him; the correct answers to all these questions also become clear to him, which the non-believer can never solve.
