For this reason, this world does not remain a place of confusion for him; rather, by the light of faith, every single thing of it is illuminated for him, and every aspect of it becomes clear to him. He takes every step in the full light of day, and in whichever direction he walks, the light of God's faith guides him. This reality is clarified in this segment: the Light of this heaven and earth is Allah—whoever has this light is in the light and on the Straight Path, and whoever is deprived of this light is wandering in a world of darkness, and no other can give him light. "Wa man lam yaj'ali Allahu lahu nooran fama lahu min noorin" [An-Noor: 40].

The parable of the light of faith inside the heart:
"Mathalu noorihi kamishkatin feeha misbahun al-misbahu fee zujajatin az-zujajatu ka-annaha kawkabun durriyyun"—this parable is being described: the light of faith entering the heart of a person adds another light over the light of his nature, by which his inner self becomes the dawn of lights. He said: "The parable of this light of faith inside the heart is as a niche in which is placed a lamp. The lamp is inside a globe, and the glass of the globe is shining like a brilliant star."

By "mishkat" (niche) is meant the heart of man, which is compared to a niche or lamp-stand. For a lamp, the rule is: it is placed at a high place in the house so that the light may spread in the entire house. Inside man, the heart is that very place where light, if present, spreads in his entire apparent and hidden. Regarding this lamp, it is said that it is closed inside a glass. The benefit of being closed inside a glass is: the flame of the lamp is not dispersed by the drafts of wind; rather, it remains concentrated on one center, which increases its brilliance. This exact same is the state of the heart from the blessing of faith. It too is protected from being double-minded. No matter what the situations are, it remains pleased and content in every state. This same heart is expressed as "the satisfied soul" (an-nafs al-mutma'innah) in the Quran, and this is the greatest wealth which man obtains because of faith.

Regarding this glass, it is said that it is like a brilliant star. If the glass is dirty, not only does it not add to the light, but it becomes a veil for it; He said: the light of faith, by purifying the heart from every kind of dirt, makes it clear and transparent like a mirror.

"Yooqadu min shajaratin mubarakatin zaytoonatin la sharqiyyatin wa-la gharbiyyatin yakadu zaytuha yudee'u wa-law lam tamsashu narun noorun 'ala noorin"...

Meaning of "Mubarakah" in its literal meaning:
"Mubarakah" (blessed) here is in its literal meaning, in the manner fertile and fresh land is called blessed by the Quran, similarly here for a fresh and fruit-bearing tree, the word "mubarakah" is used.

Characteristics of the tree of the middle of the garden:
"La sharqiyyatin wa-la gharbiyyatin"—this is the attribute of the blessed tree. That is: this tree is neither of the eastern side of the garden, nor of the western side, but of the middle of the garden. It should be kept in mind: the trees of the edges of the garden, particularly those in the east or west, because of being always in the target of sun and wind, do not yield as good fruit as the trees of the middle of the garden yield; for this reason, by adding this restriction, He pointed to the extreme freshness and fruit-bearing of this tree.

The light of faith is granted to those whose nature is correct:
This is the further attribute of this lamp: it is lit from the oil of such a fresh and fertile olive tree which is neither of the eastern side of the garden, nor of the western side, but of the middle of the garden, and its oil is so clean and transparent that it appears as though it would catch fire even without being touched by fire.

This is a pointer to the fact: this faith makes its nest in the breasts of those whose nature is protected from every kind of corruption and blemish—whose oil of nature is pure from non-natural mixtures, it catches fire spontaneously on the slightest friction of the call of faith, and in this manner, over the light of nature, from the light of faith, the breast becomes light upon light.

"Yahdee Allahu linoorihi man yasha'u"—the verb "yahdee" (He guides) when its preposition is "Li" contains also the meaning of support and success, as we have explained under Surah Yunus 35. This is a pointer to that Divine Sunnah which Allah has approved in the matter of faith and guidance, and whose explanation has passed in places: Allah illuminates the hearts of those alone with this light of faith who protect their oil of nature. Those who destroy this oil or biga-dh its original temperament by their deviation and crookedness—their hearts do not catch the light of faith. The words "la sharqiyyatin wa-la gharbiyyatin" point to this being safe from deviation and crookedness. Within these words is also a subtle allusion to that dispute of the Jews and Christians which arose between them, regarding the Qiblah, in the matter of east and west, because of which they became so far from the original middle point that they were deprived of the honor of the middle nation forever, and the success of accepting Islam could not be obtained by them. Allah opens the gates of His further support to those alone who appreciate the support granted beforehand; those who do not appreciate it, even that guidance is taken back from them which is granted through nature. The master Jesus said: he who is a thief in one penny, a trust of a hundred thousand is not entrusted to him.

"Wa yadribu Allahu al-amthala linnasi"—before "linnasi", a noun is assumed as omitted: "and for the teaching and reminder of people, Allah describes these parables, so that people may recognize the value of faith from them and illuminate their hearts with its light." Parables are the highest and most effective medium of clarifying realities; if people do not benefit even from them, this is their own deprivation!

"Wallahu bikulli shay'in 'aleem"—this points to the fact: the will of Allah is always based on His knowledge and wisdom. He is well-aware of the apparent and hidden of everyone; for this reason, He deals with everyone exactly as they deserve.

Verses 36-38:
Structure of "Fee buyootin" in the speech; the reason for mentioning temples in the parable:
"Fee buyootin..."—the connection of "fee buyootin" (in houses), in my view, is with "mishkat" (niche). This vessel has clarified: in this parable, the niche of temples of idols or wine-houses is not presented, but the niche of the temples of God is presented. In the parable, as is obvious, by "mishkat" is meant the upright heart or, in other words, the believing heart. This heart is not found in every body, but in specific bodies alone; and these bodies are not found everywhere, but their primary place is the mosques of God and His temples. Zamakhshari has preferred this statement. In his view, the actual structure of the speech is: "fee baytin min buyootin adhina Allahu an turfa'a..."

Pointer in the parable to the niches of mosques:
"Adhina Allahu an turfa'a wa yudhkara feeha ismuhu"—"raf'" here is in the meaning of building and constructing. For example: (And [remember] when Abraham was raising the foundations of the House...) [Al-Baqarah: 127], and "dhikr" here is comprehensive of prayer and all those forms of remembrance and call which are adopted for the remembrance of Allah and the supremacy of His religion. By "adhina Allahu" (Allah permitted/ordered), pointer is made to the reality: these houses were built by the command of God, so that His word may be elevated in them. Although the words are general, their primary referents are those temples which were built directly by the command of Allah—for example, the Sacred House, whose construction Allah commanded to Hazrat Ibrahim and Ismail (peace be upon them), or the Temple of Jerusalem, whose construction Hazrat Sulayman (peace be upon him) did by the permission of Allah. Then, those temples also will enter in their command which were built as their branches. As for those temples which the polytheists built in completely contrary to the permission of God, for the worship of their idols and stones, they are out of this command. Their status is of places of impurity. Their niches are not meant in this parable.

"Yusabbihu lahu feeha bil-ghuduwwi wal-asali rijalun la tulheehim tijaratun wa-la bay'un 'an dhikri Allahi wa-iqami as-salati wa-eeta'i az-zakati yakhafoona yawman tataqallabu feehi al-quloobu wal-absaru"

The mention of the effect after the cause:
In the previous verses is the picture and parable of faith. When the speech reached the mention of mosques, from there, a highly appropriate occasion was created for the mention of the carriers of faith, so that it may be clarified: those who are the carriers of this faith—what is their life. This is, as it were, the mention of the effect after the explanation of the cause, so that the reality may be مصور-ed in practical life. He said: "In those mosques and temples, whose niches are presented in the parable here, those servants of Allah glorify Him morning and afternoon, whom neither trade nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah, the establishment of prayer, and the giving of zakah, because they always fear the arrival of a day in which hearts and eyes will be in state of extreme anxiety."

Meaning of "Ghaduww and Asal":
"Ghaduww wal-asal" is spoken in Arabic in the meaning in which we say "morning and afternoon" in our language.

Meaning of "Rijal":
By "rijal" (men), the extreme praise of these people is clarified. That is: these are those men of God whom no thing of this world distracts from God. To them, dearer than everything is the pleasure of God.

Meaning of "Tijarah and Bay'":
Among "tijarah" (trade) and "bay'" (sale), the first word is in the general meaning of commercial business, and "bay'" is in the well-known meaning of selling. This is the mention of the specific after the general. If there is an opportunity to sell some thing, the trader does not want to lose this opportunity, but these servants of God are such that let alone trade, even such opportunities of greed do not distract them from God, and the true status of a believer is only: he should do trade, sale, and purchase, but must not be distracted from the remembrance of God and the performance of His obligations.

Prayer and Zakah:
The mention of prayer and Zakah has entered as the two basic obligations of religion. Under them, in reality, the entire religion is. We have explained this in another place.

Meaning of "Taqallub al-quloob wal-absar":
"Tataqallabu feehi al-quloobu wal-absaru" is a picture of the terror of Resurrection. That is: on that day, those who spent life in heedlessness of this day will find no peace. Their hearts will be flying, and this bad-mindedness of theirs will manifest from their gazes.

Verse 38:
"Li" to express the result of the action:
This "Li" is not the "Li" of cause; rather, this is that "Li" which comes to describe the end, result, and fruit of some action. For example: (And the family of Pharaoh picked him up that he might become to them an enemy and a [cause of] grief) [Al-Qasas: 8]. Imru' al-Qays has a famous poem:
"And your eyes shed tears only to strike
With your arrows into the parts of a killed heart."
In this too, ponder: "lidadribee" is for the description of the result of the action.

The meaning of the verse is: what they are doing in this world out of fear of the Hereafter, for seeking the pleasure of Allah, they will receive its reward far better than their actions, and furthermore, Allah will favor them with His grace, and those whom Allah favors with His grace, He provides for them without account—meaning, of which they will have no suspicion.

After describing the parable of faith and the carriers of faith, this is the parable of disbelief and the apparent and hidden of the people of disbelief, showing: of what degree light is there, of the same degree is darkness here. In the parable of the believers, He first highlighted the cause, i.e., faith, after that, the effect, i.e., action; and in the parable of the disbelievers, He first highlighted the effect, after that, the cause. The reason for this is: faith is a deep reality whose roots are descended inside the depths of human nature, and in reality, these are its blessed effects and fruits which manifest in the actions and morals of life. In contrast to it, disbelief is like a tree grown on a heap, whose poisonous fruits indeed are before us, but inside nature, it has no root which requires highlighting.

He said: "Those who disbelieved—their and their actions' parable is as a mirage in some flat desert, which a thirsty person assumes to be water, but when he arrives there, this secret opens upon him: what he assumed to be surging water was in reality shining and surging sand; and seeing this, he remains completely helpless, having neither path to go nor place to stay! Ultimately, he dies there, trembling with thirst." He said: "This same will be the state of these disbelievers on the Day of Resurrection. When they arrive at their actions, this secret will open upon them: all their actions have become scattered dust. However, on that day, in place of their actions, they will deal with their Lord, and He will pay their account fully, and He is swift in paying the account in the blink of an eye."

The picture of the disbelievers' deprivation on the Day of Resurrection:
From "hatta idha ja'ahu" until the end of the verse is the picture of that deprivation with which the disbelievers will deal in the Hereafter—meaning, the entire matter is: "hatta idha ja'a al-kafi'ru a'malahu yawma al-qiyamah" (until when the disbeliever arrives at his actions on the Day of Resurrection). Since in the parable, the actual purpose was to clarify the deprivation of the Hereafter, the speech turned to it without hesitation, and inside the object pronoun is the care of the represented, i.e., the mirage.

Meaning of the word "Kuffar" in the Quran:
The word "kuffar" (disbelievers) or "alladhina kafaroo" (those who disbelieved) has not been used in the Quran anywhere specifically for the deniers of God. Among the addressees of the Quran, no character was a denier of God; rather, all were polytheists. To these polytheists, the Quran has somewhere said "kuffar" and somewhere "mushrikeen", because Shirk in reality is disbelief. In religion, merely believing in God is not desired, but believing with all His attributes and all the demands of those attributes is desired. The person who does not believe in God with His attributes and their demands—all his actions will remain completely weightless on the Day of Resurrection, even if he performed them assuming them virtues.

On "Wallahu saree'u al-hisab" (And Allah is swift in account), we have discussed in another place. This has dispelled that delusion in which the foolish are involved: "Resurrection and account-keeping, even if they exist, are such far-off matters that there is no need to worry about them from now." They assume: who can keep the account of such a large world and such countless individuals! He said: those who, falling into this kind of pressure, spent their entire lives in vanity and bazz-mastee will see in the Hereafter that the entire account has come before them in the blink of an eye.

"Bahr lujjiyy" means deep sea. "Lujj" is that part of the sea where the water is deepest. "Lujjiyy" is its attribution.

The parable of the mental darkness of the disbelievers:
In the above verse was the parable of the actions of the disbelievers. Now, this is the parable of their mental darkness: because of not believing in God, or not believing correctly, they lead their life inside a deep darkness. The parable of this darkness is: there is a deep sea, the night is dark, wave after wave is rising, and above are clouds, and they are surrounded inside these darknesses, one above another. The state is: if they stretch out their hand, they cannot see it. He said: "Light is obtained from the faith of God. Whomever, because of his wretchedness, God has not given the success of faith, he cannot obtain light from anywhere. He will continue to wander in darknesses in this manner until he comes to the highway of faith, and day after day, his darknesses will increase."

The upcoming theme... Verses 41-57:
After clarifying the reality of faith and disbelief, in the forward verses is the invitation to faith in Allah. First, attention is directed toward those attributes and signs of Allah which testify: in this universe, only Allah has the sovereignty, and no other has any part in it; for this reason, let everyone hand themselves over to Him. Then, specifically, the hypocrites are warned: now their policy of divided loyalty cannot run; they must either join in the obedience of Allah and His Messenger single-mindedly, or remember: Allah will grant power to His sincere servants in this land, and all their enemies, whether hidden or open, will remain humiliated.

In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

Every single thing of this universe invites to the glorification of God through its action:
This exact same theme has passed in verse 18 of Surah Al-Hajj; there we have explained it in detail. Every single thing of this universe glorifies God, and their apparent state, which comes under our observation, bears witness: just as they are in every moment submissive to their Lord and active in His command, similarly, their hidden is also in every moment active in His glorification and sanctification. Although we cannot understand their glorification, as is said: (but you do not understand their glorification) [Al-Isra: 44], because of not being well-acquainted with their language and movements. All these things invite man, through the language of their state, to join them in this praise and glorification, and like them, worship only his Lord. If he adopts any separate path, as it were, he adopts a path completely separate from the entire world, and strikes a song which is completely unharmonious with the collective song of this universe. In this is also encouragement for those who adopt the path of God: they must not consider themselves alone or in minority. The traveler of this path is never alone. This path is filled with caravans. In it, the entire universe is his companion. If some ungrateful humans separate from it, why should he be disheartened and hopeless because of their separation, when God's heavens and earth, His sun and moon, His rivers and mountains, and all His beasts and birds are with him at all times.

Pointer to the worship of the birds in the atmosphere:
"Wat-tayru saffatin" (and the birds with wings spread)—this is a pointer to the birds among the things between the heavens and the earth: these birds too in the atmosphere, with their wings spread, glorify God. In another place, reference is made to the songs of the birds. Here, the spreading of their wings is mentioned, which is the picture of their prostration before God.

The method of worship of every thing is separate:
"Kullun qad 'alima salatahu wa-tasbeehahu"—meaning, do not assume: for every single thing of the world, God has prescribed one single method of prayer and glorification; rather, He has inspired separate methods of glorification and worship to every single thing, and every single thing has learnt the method of its prayer and glorification well, and glorifies God according to that method.

"Wallahu 'aleemun bima yaf'aloon"—meaning, if you do not know their glorification and worship, no difference occurs because of this. God is aware of all those actions of theirs which they do, and God alone is informing you of this action of theirs so that you may obtain a lesson from it.

"Wa-lillahi mulku as-samawati wal-ardi wa-ila Allahi al-maseer" [42].

The one worthy of worship is only Allah, without partners:
This is the argument: why does every single thing glorify God, and why must everyone glorify Him? He said: "The sovereignty of the heavens and the earth is God's alone, and the return of everyone ultimately is to God alone. Neither in the system of this universe does anyone have any part, nor in the Hereafter will there be any refuge and helper besides God; so, when in here and there, the entire choice and power is possessed by Him, on what right should anyone other than Him be glorified and worshipped!"

Both mercy and punishment are in God's power alone:
This is the argument: in this universe, the entire choice and power belongs to Allah alone; He alone does what He intends in it with His absolute choice. No other is associated in His wisdom. See, the earth is thirsty—no one has the power to drop a single drop of water from the sky. He alone brings the clouds, driven by His winds, and joins their separate pieces together to make them one above another. Then, He sends down rain from them to wash the entire earth. Similarly, see: His punishment is also in His power, in the manner His mercy is. For whomsoever He intends, He turns these clouds into a punishment—they appear carrying mountains of hail inside them. Then, upon whomsoever is His command, He destroys them through this hail-hailstorm, and whomsoever He intends, He saves from this calamity. No other can save himself or others from this calamity. Inside these clouds is lightning, whose flash is such that it appears to take away the sight.

"Yuqallibu Allahu al-layla wan-nahara inna fee dhalika la'ibratan li-olee al-absari" [44].

The coming and going of night and day is in the power of Allah alone:
Similarly, bringing the day after the night, and the night after the day, is in the power of Allah alone. No one has the power to manifest the day in the night, or the night in the day, or create a difference of minutes or seconds in their coming and going. He said: "Within all these matters to which pointer was made above, for those of sight, is a great lesson." The meaning of "'ibrah" we have explained: to cross over from one reality to another reality. That is: those who possess that sight which sees the hidden inside the apparent, and the universal inside the particular, will not have the slightest doubt, after these observations, that this universe is built by a Wise and Powerful God, who is the sole Sovereign and Controller of this entire universe; for this reason, He alone is worthy: His worship and obedience be done. It should be kept in mind: this "'ibrah" is the primary jewel of humanity. If this jewel is not inside someone, he is not man but beast, and the eye which cannot see the hidden inside the apparent is blind.

"The stream was not seen in the drop, and the whole in the part,
It was a game of children, the seeing eye was not there."

The variety of species is not the argument for multiple deities:
That is, the difference and variety of species, shapes, and figures is also not the argument for multiple wills being active in this universe, as the foolish polytheists assumed; rather, this is the argument for the power and wisdom of the Creator of this world. From one single water, He gave existence to all living things. Some among them walk crawling on their bellies. Some walk on two legs, and some on four legs. Who can say: the Creator of those of two legs is separate, and the God of those of four legs is separate! The Creator of all is One. He alone, by His power, creates what He intends, and gives whatever shape and figure He intends. He is powerful over everything; for this reason, everyone must hand themselves over to Him, and worship and obey Him alone.

The Divine Sunnah in the matter of guidance and error:
This is a warning of the same kind as the warning in verse 34. The meaning is: "We have sent down these eye-opening and Straight Path-showing verses. Now, under Our Sunnah, We will grant the guidance of the Straight Path to those alone who seek it. As for the ungrateful and unappreciative—they will continue to wander in the darknesses of their desires, until their end comes before their eyes." We have explained in places: God has kept His will under His Sunnah, which is absolute in the sense that no other can change it, but His every will is based on His wisdom.

Review of the attitude of the hypocrites:
After the common invitation to hand over to Allah, now He has taken the hypocrites specifically, whose trials, as clear from the trial of Ifk, had increased much in this period. He said: "As far as the verbal declaration is concerned, these too, like other Muslims, claim faith in Allah and the Messenger and claim obedience, but when the time of the trial of this claim of faith and obedience comes, they prove completely lazy."

"Wa ma ola'ika bil-mu'mineena"—meaning, such claimants of faith are not believers in the presence of Allah. The right of faith is not fulfilled merely by uttering the word; rather, it is a necessary condition: the commands of Allah and His Messenger must be obeyed without hesitation. If some person does not fulfill this condition, he is a liar in his claim and a hardcore hypocrite.

The hypocrites who turn away from the courts of the messengers:
This is the argument for their nifaq and lack of faith: "When some dispute arises among them, and they are invited to present it before Allah and the Messenger, so that the Messenger of Allah may decide according to the law of Allah, a group of them turn away." The invitation to Allah, as is obvious, is the invitation to present before the law of Allah, whose translator is the Messenger of Allah. What the Messenger of Allah decides is the decision of Allah. If some person is a claimant of faith in Allah and the Messenger, but refers his disputes to others, turning away from the court of the Messenger, he is a hypocrite.

Remember here that thing explained in detail in another place in this book: at the beginning, as long as the Jews maintained their influence inside the surroundings of Madinah, the practice of the hypocrites was: they referred their disputes to their courts. The reason for this, first: the Jews had changed the Shariah of Allah to make it according to their desires; second: their judges could easily be bought by bribery. From this, the hypocrites benefited: if they expected to get a decision according to their desire from the court of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), they brought the case to his court with great obedience; and if they did not expect so, they referred to the courts of the Jews.

That is, if they estimated that the decision of the law of Islam in the presented dispute was in their favor, they brought the matter to the court of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) with great obedience; and if they estimated that the law of Islam was contrary to their interest, they referred to the courts of the Jews. This practice of theirs has been expressed as "seeking judgment from Taghut" by the Quran, and has been declared Shirk, whose explanation we have done in its place.

Meaning of "Marad":
The word "marad" (disease), as we have explained in its place, comes in the Quran to express jealousy and also to express nifaq (hypocrisy). Here, context is proof that nifaq is meant. Both jealousy and nifaq being the disease of the heart is clear.

"Irtiyab" means to fall into doubt. This doubt can be regarding the mission of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and also regarding the future of Islam. A major cause for the double-minded policy of the hypocrites was: they feared, in this struggle hot between the Muslims and their opponents, who knows who will win the final game; so, it is wise to maintain connection with both, so that whoever wins, our interest remains protected.

Post-mortem of the policy of the hypocrites:
"Hayf" means wrong and injustice.

He said: "This double-minded policy of the hypocrites is not free of three states: either their hearts have the disease of nifaq, or they are still in doubt regarding the mission of the Messenger and the future of Islam, or they fear: if they bring their disputes to the court of Allah and the Messenger, Allah and the Messenger will do injustice to them. Now, let them decide themselves: in which of these three diseases they are involved, or are involved in all three together. Whichever is the case, it is contrary to faith."

Review of the attitude of the hypocrites:
"Bal ola'ika humu ad-dalimoona"—meaning, they are in fear of injustice from Allah and the Messenger, whereas by adopting this path, they themselves are doing wrong to their own souls—they assume they are securing their future interest, whereas they have taken refuge under a collapsing wall.

The status of a true Muslim:
This is the status of true Muslims: when they are called to Allah and His Messenger to decide some dispute, their status is: they accept it, saying, "We hear and obey." He said: "Those who, rising above every interest, fear, and greed, hand themselves over to Allah and His Messenger—indeed, they are the ones who secure their true interest in this world and the Hereafter, not those who want to maintain friendship with both disbelief and Islam. These riders of two boats will inevitably drown."

In "yattaqihi", because of abbreviation, the shape of the word has changed, but this kind of abbreviation is well-known in Arabic.

Meaning of "Khashyah" and "Taqwa":
Both "khashyah" (fear) and "taqwa" (piety) are used together here. In my view, "khashyah" is to express the state of the heart, and by "taqwa" is meant the guarding of the limits of Allah—meaning, one relates to the hidden, the other relates to the apparent.

The true successful group:
He said: "The true successful are those who, in every soft and hard situation, rising above all their personal interests, obey Allah and His Messenger, and their hearts have the fear of Allah, and they guard His limits in their daily life." From this, it is known: for success, first, the mere claim of faith is not sufficient, but along with it, the obedience of Allah and His Messenger is also a necessary condition. Second, mere obedience also is not sufficient, but along with this obedience, the fear of God and piety are also necessary. He said: "Ola'ika humu al-fa'izoon"—these are the successful ones; as for those interest-seekers who want to accompany Allah and His Messenger only to the extent of their interest, Allah and His Messenger have no connection with them.

The attitude of the hypocrites in the matter of Jihad:
Despite making the covenant of hearing and obeying, the manner in which the hypocrites turned away from the court of the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), they turned away also from Jihad. In terms of verbal loquacity, the state was: when Jihad was mentioned, they swore strong oaths to assure the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that whenever he commanded, they were ready with life and wealth; but when the time came, they retreated. He said: "Tell them: do not swear many oaths; oaths cannot stand in place of action. The actual thing desired is the obedience of Allah and His Messenger according to the principles. If this is not present, how many days will you put a veil on your character through empty oaths!"

"Inna Allaha khabeerun bima ta'maloona"—meaning, through these false oaths, you can deceive others, but how can you deceive the Knower of the Unseen, who is well-aware of your every word and deed.

The mention of the hypocrites swearing many oaths has passed in other places in the Quran, and will come forward too. We have explained its psychological cause: those who do not have the testimony of character make oaths the medium to make themselves credible. Within their hearts, this thief is hidden: until they swear an oath, the addressee will not believe their word. The mature and possessor of character has trust in his character; for this reason, he does not face the need to swear oaths.

Warning and advice to the hypocrites:
This is a warning to the hypocrites: "Obey Allah and obey the Messenger. If you turn away from this, remember: you will not harm the Messenger, but will destroy yourselves. In your case, the responsibility upon the Messenger is only what has been placed on him by Allah—meaning, he should clearly convey the religion of God to you."

So, he has performed this duty of his with goodness and beauty. now, this responsibility is not upon him to make you walk on the path of faith and guidance too. The responsibility to adopt the path of faith and guidance is upon you, and in the presence of God, you have to answer why you did not believe the Messenger and why you did not adopt the path of guidance.

"Wa-in tutee'oohu tahtadoo"—within this phrase, there is both threat and advice. That is: "Obey the Messenger, you will find success through this alone, otherwise remember: you will invite your own doom."

The responsibility of the messenger is only clear notification:
"Wa ma 'ala ar-rasooli illa al-balaghu al-mubeenu"—this has clarified the responsibility of the Messenger: "The responsibility upon him from God is only to clearly convey the religion of God to people. After this, if you do not perform your responsibility, you will suffer its consequence."

Faith's necessary requirement is obedience:
In these verses, see: repeatedly, faith's necessary requirement is stated as the obedience of Allah and the Messenger, and the path to the obedience of Allah is established as the obedience of the Messenger. Without this, if the right of faith had been fulfilled merely by saying "Amanna billahi wa bir-rasooli", these hypocrites were not behind anyone in this declaration. They indeed declared "Amanna" along with "Ata'na", and also swore paka oaths on the truth of their claim. Then, why did they remain worthy of the lowest depth of the Fire!

Glad tidings of victory and consolidation for the followers of the Messenger:
This has struck a blow at that doubt of the hypocrites which they held in their hearts regarding the future of Islam, and which has been pointed to by the words "wa yakhafoona" above. He said: "Those of you who adopt the path of faith and righteous action with complete uprightness—the promise of Allah has been made to them: Allah will grant them power in this land, in the manner He granted power to the nations of the previous messengers, and He will establish their religion, which He has approved for them, in this land, and will change this state of their fear into security."

Regarding this promise, its mention has passed in detail in Surah Al-Anbiya, Surah Al-Hajj, and Surah Al-Mu'minoon.

By "kama istakhlafa alladhina min qablihim", pointer is made to the previous messengers and their nations. We have referenced that Divine Sunnah in the case of the messengers: for the messenger and his followers, victory in this world is necessary. After the completion of proof of the messenger, his deniers were inevitably destroyed, and the rein of leadership and power came after them into the hands of those who believed in the messenger.

"Wa layumakkinanna lahum deenahum alladhee artada lahum"—meaning, this Islam which Allah has approved for them, as is said: (and I approved for you Islam as a religion) [Al-Ma'idah: 3], He will consolidate this religion in this land. That is: the law of this religion alone will run in this land, and on its foundations, the social and political system of this land will be established. No other religion's sovereignty will remain in this land besides this religion.

"Wa layubaddilannahum min ba'di khawfihim amnan"—meaning, this state of fear which is present currently because of the enemies of this religion is a temporary state. Soon, that time is coming when Allah will change this state of fear into security, and from one end of this land to another, no one will have the capability to create any danger for the religion of Allah or its carriers.

"Ya'budoonanee la yushrikoona bee shay'an"—meaning, on this land, only My worship will be done, and no other's worship will be done besides Me.

This promise of Allah, the manner in which it was fulfilled after the victory, is a historical reality which no one can deny. To the extent that regarding the entire Arabian Peninsula, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) announced: "Two religions cannot coexist in it."

"Wa man kafara ba'da dhalika fa-ola'ika humu al-fasiqoon"—meaning, today, since it is still the stage of trial and examination, for this reason, respite is given to those too who are opposing the religion of God; but forward, the hour of decision is also coming; at that time, those who are disobeying Allah and His Messenger will see their end. By "fa-ola'ika humu al-fasiqoon" (then those are the disobedient), the purpose is to point to its consequence.

This is the remedy shown: if you want to keep your hearts pure from the diseases of nifaq, fear, and doubt, care for prayer, give Zakah, and obey the Messenger's every command without hesitation. If you adopt this path, there will be mercy of Allah upon you, and you will be successful in both this world and the Hereafter.

The awe of the opponents of Islam, particularly the Quraysh, which was on the hearts of the hypocrites, is removed here: "Do not think of these disbelievers that they will go out of Our grasp. They are in Our grasp in this land too, and in the Hereafter, there is indeed Hell for them, and remember: it is an extremely bad abode."

The upcoming theme... Verses 58-64:
Forward are the concluding verses of the Surah. Regarding the directions given in verses 27-31 of this Surah in the case of the veil, those questions and doubts which arose in some people's minds later have been explained in these verses. These verses, since they were sent down after the original commands, were kept at the end of the Surah, and along with them, this specification too is present: these verses were sent down as explanations.

Finally, three verses relate to the hypocrites, whose mention ended in verse 57. From the context, it is known: these verses too were sent down as further warnings later, because of which they were given place at the end alongside the verses sent down later, so that their full importance might be clear. In this light, recite the verses.

Verses 58-59:
Restriction of seeking permission during three times for slaves and children:
Take a look back at verse 31. There, from the condition of seeking permission to enter houses, slaves and immature children were kept exempt. Later, this restriction was imposed on them too: let them also seek permission during three times. Before the Dawn prayer (Fajr), at noon when you take off your clothes for qayloolah (midday nap), and after the Night prayer ('Isha). The reason for this special caution during these three times is: these are times of informality and being uncovered. The times before Fajr and after 'Isha being times of being uncovered is clear. At noon too, it is generally time of rest. Particularly in Arabia, because of being a hot country, people take a midday nap at this time. In these times, the entering of slaves and children without permission was subject to the possibility: they might see the household in some state in which seeing is not liked. For this reason, this restriction was imposed, and in imposing these restrictions, a gradual development was considered, so that they might not be highly hard on natures. Except during these three times, there is no restriction of seeking permission on them. Its wisdom is clarified: (they are constant visitors among you, some of you around others) [An-Noor: 58]; for this reason, Allah did not like to put you in any difficulty.

"Wa-idha balagha al-atfalu minkumu al-huluma..."—but these immature children too are exempt from the restriction of seeking permission only as long as they are immature. After becoming mature, all those restrictions will be imposed on them too which are mentioned for others. On the basis of the argument: they have been coming and going in the house since childhood, no favor will be shown to them.

Both these verses are explanatory:
With both these verses, see: this specification is present: these were sent down as explanations of the previous commands, and this explanation is based on the knowledge and wisdom of God. Allah alone knows what commands must be given for His servants' training and reform, and in what order and gradual development they must be sent down.

A concession for old women:
This verse also is among the explanatory verses. Above in verse 31, in the directions given to women in the presence of any non-relative inside the house, this direction is also present: "wal-yadribna bikhumurihinna 'ala juyoobihinna"—let them cover their bosoms with the onch-als of their head-coverings. From this common command, those women who have passed the age of marriage are exempted here. That is: if they do not maintain this care of the head-covering, there is no great harm, on the condition: they are not displayers of decoration. Although the preferred thing for them too is: they also maintain that caution which is described for other women. For old women, this concession has been kept for their ease, but in this also, is an aspect of trial. Just as the state of some men is: they become old but the nose remains, similarly, some weakness is inside some women: they become old but the desire for displaying their ornaments and wealth does not leave them. Since this is in itself a trial for a person's religion, attention is directed to it: let them not take any wrong advantage of this concession.

"Wallahu samee'un 'aleemun" (And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing) is here for warning—meaning, everyone must remember: no word remains hidden from God. He hears all and knows all.

Verse 61:
The purpose of the veil is not the disruption of social relations:
This verse also is among the explanatory verses. When those restrictions on entering houses were imposed which have been described above, it appears: some people felt: Islam wants to restrict social freedoms—that even in one's close, relative, and friends' houses, a person cannot come and go with freedom and informality now. Many people who were disabled and forced, and because of their helplessness, survived on their relatives' houses, also felt: now their freedom has become restricted. In the era of ignorance, because of being given unrestricted freedom, in contrast to it, even this much restriction was hard on some natures. To remove the doubt of such people, this explanation was made: by imposing some cautionary restrictions, neither is it meant that the blind, lame, and sick should remain deprived of their relatives' support, nor is it meant that social relations with relatives and friends should be disrupted. People may come, go, meet, eat, and drink. Individually too, and socially in gatherings too. On these things is no restriction; what is desired is: when people enter houses, enter offering greeting. This is a blessed and pure method of greeting and prayer from Allah, which increases the comfort of mutual relationships and closes the rungs of Satan's feni-makings. The meaning is: do not consider this restriction hard. In it is entirely your own success and prosperity.

Meaning of "Aw ma malaktum mafatihahu":
By "aw ma malaktum mafatihahu" (or what you possess the keys of), in my view, is meant those houses whose custodianship is upon a person. For example, some person is the guardian of some orphan.

"Kadhalika yubayyinu Allahu lakum al-ayati la'allakum ta'qiloon"—this verse points: this is, as we have explained, the explanation of some previous command. From "la'allakum ta'qiloon" (that perhaps you will understand), the point emerges: those who considered these directions as unlawful restrictions did so merely because of misunderstanding. The Quran made this explanation so that the misunderstanding of such people may be removed and they may understand the utility and blessing of these commands well.

In this verse, the separate mention of all relatives and close ones with this specification is because: the common family system was not current in Arabia; rather, every mature person settled his own house after marriage, and from this specification, that point also emerged to which we have pointed: the veil under discussion in this Surah is that which men and women must maintain in the case of entering and exiting the houses of relatives and trusted ones. Here is no explanation of that veil which women must maintain in the case of stepping out of the house. Its explanation, as we have pointed out, is in Surah Al-Ahzab, and we will discuss it in detail there. Anyway, we have written a separate treatise under the title 'The Quran and the Veil' too, in which we have clarified in the light of the Quran: for both inside and outside situations, the Quran has given highly decisive commands of the veil. Those seeking details may read that treatise. Even after these clear commands, those who do not see the command of the veil anywhere in the Quran—what can be said regarding them except that the lowering of the gaze the Quran directed from non-relative women, these gentlemen have done from the Quran itself! These people kiss the Quran with great devotion, but with closed eyes, and after kissing, leave it, considering it a heavy stone!

Warning to the hypocrites who retreat from collective works:
This and the subsequent verses relate to that mention of the hypocrites which ended in verse 57. From the context, it is known: these verses were sent down as further warnings later, because of which they were given place alongside the verses sent down later, so that their full importance might be clear. He said: "The actual believers are those who believe in Allah and His Messenger with a true and mature faith." Context is proof that the verb "amanoo" (they believed) here is used in its complete meaning. "Wa-idha kanoo ma'ahu 'ala amrin jami'in"—this is a sign of their mature faith described: when they are with the Messenger for some collective work, they do not do like the hypocrites—who, when they wished, retreated from there silently; rather, if they have any necessity, they retreat only after seeking permission from the Messenger. He said: "Those who seek permission are the true believers." The meaning is: those who violate this are not believers, but are paka hypocrites. He said: "Those who are seekers of permission for some real necessity, among them, whomever you wish, give permission, and seek forgiveness of Allah for them." From this, it is known: although seeking such permission is not a preferred thing, because no other work can be greater than the company and help of the Messenger, particularly when some collective necessity is before them, yet keeping in view the human weaknesses, the Messenger was given this permission: if he wants to give permission to anyone, he can give, and seek forgiveness for his this weakness.

Although the address, in terms of words, is general, context is proof: the face of speech is toward the hypocrites alone. He said: "If the Messenger invites for some collective work, do not consider it like the calling of others; rather, everyone's duty is to present himself in his service on his every call, saying, 'We hear and obey.'"

"Qad ya'lamu Allahu alladhina yatasallaloona minkum liwadhan"—before the imperfect verb, here a past-tense helping verb is assumed as omitted. "Tasallul" means to walk away and retreat silently. "Liwadhan" means taking shelter and protection behind one another.

This is a warning to the hypocrites: "Allah has been aware of those who have been retreating silently from the assemblies called by the Messenger, taking shelter behind one another, so that if questioned, they may make this excuse: they did not find out the command of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)." The anger and wrath implied in this style of expression is not hidden from those who possess taste for the language.

"Falyahdhari alladhina yukhalifoona 'an amrihi..."—"mukhalafah" (opposition) here contains the meaning of evasion and flight; for this reason, its preposition has come with "'an". The nature of the opponents' opposition, in reality, was evasion and flight. They said "Amanna" and "Ata'na" before him, but in the hidden, tried for evasion and flight. He said: "Those who have adopted this path must not forget: in its consequence, they can be tested with some very great trial, and also can be caught in some painful punishment."

This is the final warning to the hypocrites in this Surah. He said: "Hear well with open ears: whatever is in the heavens and the earth is God's alone; no other has any part in it. In whatever state you have been, or are, Allah has been aware of it and is." After this, turning His face from them, He said: "These people must remember that day when their presence will be before Allah, and Allah will place their entire record before them. Allah is aware of everything."

On these lines, by the support of Allah, the commentary of this final Surah of this group is completed. May Allah forgive the slips, and create place in the hearts for the correct words. Praise be to Allah for this.
